relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The Divine called Jacob back to Bethel, back to the place of flight, vow, and encounter.
Jacob told the household to remove foreign gods, purify, and change garments.
He remembered the Divine who answered him in distress and walked with him on the way.
The household surrendered its foreign gods, and Jacob buried them under the tree near Shechem.
A divine terror protected them from pursuit.
Jacob and all his people came to Bethel in Canaan.
He built the altar and named the place El-Bethel, honoring the revelation in his flight.
Deborah died and was buried under the oak of weeping.
The Divine appeared again and blessed Jacob.
Jacob’s Israel-name was reaffirmed.
El Shaddai spoke fruitfulness, nations, and kings over him.
The land promise of Abraham and Isaac was given again to Jacob and his seed.
The Divine went up from the place of speech.
Jacob marked the place with stone, drink offering, and oil.
He called the place Bethel.
On the road from Bethel, Rachel entered hard labor.
The midwife told her not to fear; another son was being born.
As Rachel died, she named the child from sorrow, but Jacob named him Benjamin.
Rachel was buried on the way to Bethlehem.
Jacob raised a pillar over Rachel’s burial place.
Israel journeyed beyond the tower of Eder.
A grave household violation was reported, and Israel heard it; the twelve sons were now named as a whole.
Leah’s sons were listed.
Rachel’s sons were listed.
Bilhah’s sons were listed.
Zilpah’s sons were listed, completing the sons born in Paddan-Aram.
Jacob returned to Isaac at Hebron, where the fathers had sojourned.
Isaac’s years reached one hundred eighty.
Isaac died old and full of days, and Esau and Jacob buried him together.
